one_line_takeaway: Off-the-shelf machine-unlearning algorithms (best: gradient ascent + retain-set gradient descent) can cut direct PII leakage in code LLMs by 50-85% with minimal Pass@1 loss, but they merely convert explicit leakage into harder-to-detect indirect leakage of related attributes, so they are a partial rather than complete privacy fix.
key_finding: Machine unlearning substantially reduces direct memorization leakage while largely preserving utility: across models the leak rate drops by >50% on average (e.g., CodeLlama-34B 32.7%->5.0% with GA+GD, an 84.7% cut; CodeQwen-14B 26.2%->4.7%, 82.1%) while retaining ~91%+ of code-generation performance (Pass@1 drops are small, e.g., CodeQwen-14B 74.5->73.3, CodeLlama-13B 72.5->71.6). GA+GD is consistently the best privacy/utility trade-off. Larger models leak more at baseline (34B highest at 32.7%). The central caveat: unlearning shifts leakage from direct to indirect - as direct leakage ratio falls the indirect ratio rises across every model (e.g., AIXCoder-7B direct 0.49->0.38 / indirect 0.51->0.62 under GA+KL; CodeQwen-14B direct 0.45->0.30 / indirect ->0.70), meaning the model stops emitting the exact queried secret but leaks other sensitive attributes of the same person.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first systematic empirical study of applying machine unlearning to mitigate sensitive-information (PII) leakage in Large Language Models for Code (LLMs4Code) while preserving code-generation ability. Its own contributions are: (1) a dedicated dual benchmark pairing a synthetic 'forget set' of privacy-related code-completion queries with a 'retain set' of code-generation tasks; (2) an empirical evaluation of three existing unlearning algorithms (GA, GA+GD, GA+KL) across eight LLMs4Code variants, measured jointly by a privacy Leak Rate and code Pass@1; and (3) the finding that unlearning shifts leakage from 'direct' (explicitly-queried attribute) to 'indirect' (contextually-related but unqueried attribute), an under-explored residual vulnerability. The unlearning algorithms themselves (gradient ascent, GA+gradient-descent, GA+KL-divergence) are prior work adapted from the general-LLM/NLP literature (Liu et al. 2022; Rafailov et al. 2024; TOFU/Maini et al. 2024); the paper's novelty is the code-specific benchmark, the joint privacy-vs-utility evaluation, and the direct-to-indirect leakage analysis, not the unlearning methods.
threat_model: Training-data memorization leakage: LLMs4Code memorize PII/secrets (names, emails, phones, addresses, passwords, bank balances, credit cards, political affiliation) present in training data and reproduce them when prompted with code-completion 'privacy queries'. The adversary/user issues a crafted code-completion prompt (e.g., a partially-filled function referencing a person's ID) that elicits memorized sensitive fields. The defender is the model owner, who applies post-hoc machine unlearning to make the model 'forget' the sensitive associations without retraining from scratch and without degrading code-generation utility. The paper measures leakage under a strict criterion (any sensitive info in output = leak) and distinguishes direct leakage (the explicitly-requested attribute appears) from indirect leakage (a different sensitive attribute of the same person appears).
limitations: The evaluation dataset is fully synthetic (GPT-4-generated fictional resumes, TOFU-style) and may not reflect real-world code/PII complexity, likely underestimating true risk; the authors flag real GitHub/permissioned data as future work. Leak Rate depends on subjective human labeling (5 evaluators), introducing potential bias despite consensus resolution. Leakage triggering used a fixed set of crafted prompts and does not exhaust the adversarial query space (no red-teaming/black-box probing). The headline direct-leak reductions and 'preserved utility' claims are somewhat inconsistently reported (abstract says >50% average and >91% retention; discussion says 'up to 72%' reduction with '>95%' accuracy; conclusion says '~80%'), and the reduction percentages depend on which model/method is cited. The approach's own core weakness is that it does not eliminate indirect leakage and may amplify it, so it is not a complete privacy defense. The paper reports only single-run point estimates (no variance/statistical testing). Also, the abstract names three models but Table 1 actually spans eight variants.

datasets_used: Forget set: synthetic TOFU-style dataset of 500 fictional character resumes, 10 privacy questions each (5K queries), reformatted into Python code-completion tasks via GPT-4
Retain set: 5K code-generation samples drawn from HumanEval, MBXP, and LiveBench
Both sets split 9:1 into train (unlearning) and test (evaluation); Leak Rate and Pass@1 each computed on 500 test samples
quantitative_results: CodeLlama-34B leak rate: 32.7% zero-shot -> 5.0% with GA+GD (84.7% reduction); Pass@1 75.8% -> 74.6%
CodeQwen-14B leak rate: 26.2% zero-shot -> 4.7% with GA+GD (82.1% reduction); Pass@1 74.5% -> 73.3%
CodeLlama-7B leak rate: 28.6% zero-shot -> 5.6% with GA+GD; Pass@1 69.3% -> 68.8%
CodeLlama-13B leak rate: 30.4% zero-shot -> 5.2% with GA+GD; Pass@1 72.5% -> 71.6%
CodeQwen-1.5B leak rate: 17.8% zero-shot -> 7.2% with GA+GD; CodeQwen-3B: 20.5% -> 6.8% with GA+GD

Direct-to-indirect leakage shift (ratio): AIXCoder-7B direct 0.49->0.38, indirect 0.51->0.62 (GA+KL); CodeQwen-14B direct 0.45->0.30, indirect 0.55->0.70 (GA+KL); CodeQwen-7B direct 0.48->0.33, indirect 0.52->0.67

RQ2 post-unlearning privacy-protection forms (proportion): variable-name/abbreviation placeholder 17.23%, return-variable 15.02%, repetition of known info 12.52%, symbolic placeholders 11.48%, constructing answers from known info 10.81%, blank output 10.50%, skipping sensitive fields 8.65%, responding with uncertainty 7.55%, explaining sensitive field 6.24%

RQ4 fine-grained per-attribute: Email in CodeLlama 6.01% zero-shot -> 1.28% (GA) -> 0.77% (GA+GD); Password in CodeQwen 3.34% -> 0.41% (GA) -> 0.23% (GA+GD); Address in AIXCoder 2.18% (GA+KL) vs 0.56% (GA+GD); Bank balance in CodeQwen 2.86% -> 0.45% (GA+GD)
baselines_compared: Zero-shot original (un-unlearned) model as the leakage/utility baseline
GA+GD (gradient ascent + retain-set gradient descent) - prior technique, best overall trade-off
GA+KL (gradient ascent + KL-divergence regularization to a retain-set SFT reference model) - prior technique
Model families compared: AIXCoder-7B, CodeLlama-7B/13B/34B, CodeQwen-1.5B/3B/7B/14B (8 variants)
